PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.


## Change requested
The app currently FAILS to compile. Fix every compiler error until the build succeeds cleanly; do not drop features unless unavoidable.

phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Given Codex didn't weigh in, the call rests on Claude's round-1 analysis, and it's the right one: the risk here isn't finding the compiler errors (that's mechanical), it's that "fix every error" invites deleting the parts of the app that make it FaveOff. Locking in guardrails now, before anyone touches code, is more valuable than re-litigating priority order.

## Final Output

Fix the build with this priority order, and don't let anything below outrank the item above it:

1. **Ranking engine and Taste Card flow are untouchable.** The SwiftData model for `Title`/`Comparison`/ranking state and the ImageRenderer/ShareLink poster-grid generation must compile *as real, working code* — not stubbed, not type-weakened to `Any`, not force-unwrapped past a crash. If an error traces back to the `@Model` schema (e.g., an enum-with-associated-values property, a computed Elo score marked `@Attribute` instead of `@Transient`, a missing inverse on a relationship), fix the model correctly. A shallow patch that compiles but produces a broken merge-sort/Elo convergence is not an acceptable fix — it's the "comparison fatigue" failure mode from the spec, just caused by us instead of the algorithm.
2. **WidgetKit is droppable.** It's explicitly the least load-bearing of the three iOS-native features. If the small widget target is the blocker (App Group entitlement issues, target membership problems), stub or remove the widget extension rather than spend the fix budget there. Note it as dropped, don't silently delete it.
3. **StoreKit/paywall may be simplified but not gutted.** If product-ID configuration is blocking the build, it's fine to hardcode placeholder product IDs or stub the store-connect call, but the paywall UI and gating logic must remain intact and compiling.
4. **Deliverable from the fix pass:** a list of actual compiler errors with root cause per error (not just "fixed"), plus an explicit call-out of anything stubbed, disabled, or type-weakened to reach green — so this is auditable, not just trusted.
5. **Definition of done is not `xcodebuild succeeded`.** The next phase must actually launch the app and walk add-title → head-to-head compare → ranked list → Taste Card share once. A clean compile that crashes on first launch (e.g., SwiftData migration failure from a schema change mid-fix) is not done.

Net: fix the real errors, keep the widget expendable, keep the ranking engine and Taste Card sacred, and verify by running the app, not just by trusting the compiler.

### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex returned empty output — refusing to fabricate a response. See logs/._

**Claude (Product Strategist · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Since a compile fix is mechanical work best done by whoever actually touches the code, my job here is to draw the line around what "fix the build" is allowed to sacrifice — and I think the biggest risk in this phase is scope creep disguised as necessity.

The trap with "fix every compiler error" instructions is that the fastest path to green is almost always deletion: comment out the WidgetKit target because the App Group entitlement is fussy, stub out ImageRenderer poster-grid generation because it's fighting with generics, gut the StoreKit paywall because product IDs aren't configured. Every one of those "fixes" is individually defensible and collectively guts the app's actual differentiation. FaveOff's entire pitch is the head-to-head ranking engine converging to a real top 10 and the shareable Taste Card — if the fix pass quietly neuters ImageRenderer/ShareLink to get a clean build, we'll have "compiles" but not "is FaveOff." I want it explicit: widget can be dropped or stubbed if it's the blocker (it's the least load-bearing of the three iOS-native features listed), but the ranking engine and the Taste Card share flow are the product and must survive the fix intact, even if that means the fixer has to actually resolve a SwiftData/Observation/generic-constraint error instead of routing around it.

Second thing I'd flag as a skeptic: compile errors in a SwiftData-backed app are frequently symptoms of a deeper modeling mistake, not a syntax typo — things like a `@Model` class with a property type SwiftData can't persist (e.g., storing an enum with associated values, or a computed Elo score marked `@Attribute` when it should be `@Transient`), or a `Comparison`/`Title` relationship that's inverse-less and creates ambiguity. If the errors trace back to the ranking algorithm's data model, a shallow fix (force-unwrap, `as!`, silencing a type mismatch by weakening a type to `Any`) will compile but quietly break ranking correctness later — the exact "comparison fatigue" failure mode already called out as a key risk in the spec, except now caused by a broken merge-sort rather than a bad algorithm. I'd rather the fix pass take an extra round to fix the model correctly than get a green build that produces a nonsensical top 10 on first use.

Third: since this is a portfolio app competing against four siblings for "does this feel finished," I'd push back on any temptation to mark this done once `xcodebuild` succeeds. A build that compiles but crashes on first launch (e.g., SwiftData migration failure because the schema changed mid-fix) is not a win — the phase after this one needs to actually launch it and walk the add-title → compare → ranked-list → Taste Card path once, not just trust the compiler.

What I want preserved out of this phase's output: a list of the actual compiler errors with root cause per error (not just "fixed"), and an explicit call-out of anything that got stubbed, disabled, or type-weakened to reach green, so the next phase can decide whether that's acceptable or needs a real fix.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Given Codex didn't weigh in, the call rests on Claude's round-1 analysis, and it's the right one: the risk here isn't finding the compiler errors (that's mechanical), it's that "fix every error" invites deleting the parts of the app that make it FaveOff. Locking in guardrails now, before anyone touches code, is more valuable than re-litigating priority order.

2. **WidgetKit is droppable.** It's explicitly the least load-bearing of the three iOS-native features. If the small widget target is the blocker (App Group entitlement issues, target membership problems), stub or remove the widget extension rather than spend the fix budget there. Note it as dropped, don't silently delete it.
3. **StoreKit/paywall may be simplified but not gutted.** If product-ID configuration is blocking the build, it's fine to hardcode placeholder product IDs or stub the store-connect call, but the paywall UI and gating logic must remain intact and compiling.

phase_discussions.output:
Both independent reviews are in — the frontend lane's own-lane audit and my fresh cross-check of every Model/Repository/Service/Support file — and both came back clean. Combined with my own check of `project.pbxproj` (all 61 files correctly registered, no orphaned widget target) and the entry point/composition root, I have enough to close this out.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**What's in app_build now:** a complete FaveOff Xcode project — SwiftUI + SwiftData, 55 files in the main target, 6 in the test target, all correctly registered in `project.pbxproj`. Every layer is present: `Title`/`Comparison`/`InsertionContext` SwiftData models, a real `RankingEngine`, `InsertionCoordinator` driving the head-to-head comparison flow, `TasteCardRenderer` using `ImageRenderer` for the poster-grid share card, `StoreKitPurchaseService` wired to a `PaywallView`, and `LibraryView` as the root screen wired through `FaveOffApp` → `AppEnvironment` composition root.

**What I verified this round:**
- The frontend lane re-audited its entire lane (Navigation, ViewModels, Views, Components, tests) against every signature it depends on and found zero mismatches — no files changed, none needed.
- I had a fresh, independent agent read every file in Models/, Repositories/, Services/, Support/, and App/ end-to-end (30 files) specifically hunting for the failure modes the earlier guardrail phase warned about: SwiftData persistability violations, missing relationship inverses, force-unwraps/`as!`/`Any`-weakening used as shortcut fixes, and StoreKit 2 API misuse. Result: none found. All `@Model` properties are persistable types, no associated-value enums stored directly, cross-links use plain `UUID`s by design (not `@Relationship`, so no inverse issue), no `as!`/`try!`/force-unwraps anywhere in that set, and `StoreKitPurchaseService` uses correct iOS 17 StoreKit 2 (`Product.products(for:)`, `Transaction.updates`, `VerificationResult`, etc.).
- I personally checked `project.pbxproj` line by line: every file on disk has a matching `PBXFileReference` and `PBXBuildFile` entry in the right build phase, no orphaned WidgetKit extension target (it was dropped cleanly per the earlier guardrail decision, not half-deleted).

phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

The record here is clean and the fix was genuinely minimal: both compiler failures traced to `project.pbxproj` path-duplication bugs (a doubled `FaveOff/` prefix, first on `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` alone, then across the entire `FaveOff` and `FaveOffTests` groups) — not to anything in Swift source. Two independent full-source audits (frontend lane re-checking its own lane, integrator checking Models/Repositories/Services/Support/App) both came back with zero source-level defects, and the authoritative verification result confirms `xcodebuild` now compiles cleanly for the iOS Simulator after 2 repairs. So the guardrails set up earlier (protect the SwiftData model, protect ImageRenderer/ShareLink, don't force-unwrap or type-weaken) were never actually tested against a real threat — nothing in source needed to change, and nothing was stubbed, dropped, or weakened. The widget was already dropped in an earlier phase per pre-approval, and the paywall was untouched.

What's honestly still missing, and has been flagged twice now by the product-strategist voice in this discussion: nobody has launched the app in Simulator and walked the actual golden path (add title → head-to-head compare → ranked list → Taste Card share + ShareLink). A green compile proves the project file and source are structurally sound; it doesn't prove the Elo/merge-sort convergence produces a sane top 10, that the poster-grid renders legibly, or that the paywall gates correctly at runtime. That gap is real and should be stated plainly rather than smoothed over.

## Final Output

**What was built / changed this phase:** Nothing in Swift source. The only changes were two `project.pbxproj` fixes: (1) removed a duplicated `FaveOff/` path prefix on the `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` file reference, (2) removed the same duplicated `FaveOff/`/`FaveOffTests/` prefix from every file reference in those two groups. Root cause both times: Xcode was concatenating group path + file path, producing nonexistent doubled paths ("Build input file cannot be found").

**Verification status:** VERIFIED — `xcodebuild` compiled cleanly for the iOS Simulator (attempt 2, after 2 repairs). This is a real compiler-run result, not just a manual audit.

**Prompt coverage:** Full v1 build scope remains intact — SwiftData model (`Title`/`Comparison`/`InsertionContext`), real `RankingEngine`/`InsertionCoordinator` driving head-to-head comparisons, `TasteCardRenderer` (ImageRenderer) + ShareLink for the poster-grid Taste Card, `StoreKitPurchaseService` + `PaywallView` for FaveOff+. WidgetKit was dropped in an earlier phase (pre-approved, least load-bearing of the three iOS-native features) — noted, not hidden.

**Known limitations:**
- No live Simulator walkthrough has been performed in any phase of this fix. The core loop (add title → compare → ranked list → Taste Card share) has been verified only by static source audit + compiler pass, not by actually running it.
- Two rounds of manual full-source review found no defects, but that's a different guarantee than runtime behavior — Elo convergence quality, poster-grid legibility, and paywall gating are all unverified at runtime.

**Top follow-ups (in priority order):**
1. **Owner: whoever has an interactive session with Simulator access.** Open `FaveOff.xcodeproj`, run on iOS Simulator, add 2-3 titles, run one comparison round, confirm the ranked list updates sensibly, generate and share a Taste Card, and check the paywall trigger. This is the single concrete gap between "compiles" and "done" per this project's own definition of done set two phases ago.
2. No source-level follow-up is required — the fix pass didn't touch the ranking engine, Taste Card flow, or paywall, so there's nothing there to re-audit beyond the runtime check above.

**Bottom line:** Go, with one open item. The build genuinely works now (verified, not assumed), the fix was surgical and didn't compromise anything the earlier guardrail phase cared about protecting, and the app is ready to hand to a human for the five-minute Simulator smoke test that's been outstanding since the fix-scope phase. Call it ship-ready pending that one manual check, not unconditionally done.

- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.

Locking in guardrails now, before anyone touches code, is more valuable than re-litigating priority order.

## Final Output

Fix the build with this priority order, and don't let anything below outrank the item above it:

1. **Ranking engine and Taste Card flow are untouchable.** The SwiftData model for `Title`/`Comparison`/ranking state and the ImageRenderer/ShareLink poster-grid generation must compile *as real, working code* — not stubbed, not type-weakened to `Any`, not force-unwrapped past a crash. If an error traces back to the `@Model` schema (e.g., an enum-with-associated-values property, a computed Elo score marked `@Attribute` instead of `@Transient`, a missing inverse on a relationship), fix the model correctly. A shallow patch that compiles but produces a broken merge-sort/Elo convergence is not an acceptable fix — it's the "comparison fatigue" failure mode from the spec, just caused by us instead of the algorithm.
2. **WidgetKit is droppable.** It's explicitly the least load-bearing of the three iOS-native features. If the small widget target is the blocker (App Group entitlement issues, target membership problems), stub or remove the widget extension rather than spend the fix budget there. Note it as dropped, don't silently delete it.
3. **StoreKit/paywall may be simplified but not gutted.** If product-ID configuration is blocking the build, it's fine to hardcode placeholder product IDs or stub the store-connect call, but the paywall UI and gating logic must remain intact and compiling.
4. **Deliverable from the fix pass:** a list of actual compiler errors with root cause per error (not just "fixed"), plus an explicit call-out of anything stubbed, disabled, or type-weakened to reach green — so this is auditable, not just trusted.

A clean compile that crashes on first launch (e.g., SwiftData migration failure from a schema change mid-fix) is not done.

Net: fix the real errors, keep the widget expendable, keep the ranking engine and Taste Card sacred, and verify by running the app, not just by trusting the compiler.

interfaces.name: TitleType
interfaces.signature: enum TitleType: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Sendable { case movie, tv }
interfaces.notes: Track boundary is by title type; movie is free ranked track, TV ranked only with entitlement.

interfaces.name: RankingState
interfaces.signature: enum RankingState: String, Codable, Sendable { case unranked, insertionInProgress, ranked }
interfaces.notes: Uncontested across both rounds.

interfaces.name: Genre
interfaces.signature: enum Genre: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Sendable { case action, comedy, drama, horror, thriller, sciFi, fantasy, animation, documentary, romance, other }
interfaces.notes: Fixed single-pick enum, never free text.

interfaces.name: FilmTitle
interfaces.signature: struct FilmTitle: Identifiable, Equatable, Hashable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var name: String; var type: TitleType; var year: Int?; var genre: Genre?; var posterSeed: String; var rankingState: RankingState; var trackRankPosition: Int?; var createdAt: Date; var yearDecade: Int? { ... } }
interfaces.notes: Domain projection used across layers; keep separate from SwiftData persistence model.

interfaces.name: StoredTitle
interfaces.signature: struct StoredTitle: Codable, Sendable { let id: UUID; var name: String; var typeRaw: String; var year: Int?; var genreRaw: String?; var posterSeed: String; var rankingStateRaw: String; var position: Int; var insertedAt: Date }
interfaces.notes: SwiftData entity shape serialized through repository; defaults on all non-optional fields.

interfaces.name: ComparisonRecord
interfaces.signature: struct ComparisonRecord: Identifiable, Sendable { let id: UUID; let flowID: UUID; let winnerID: UUID; let loserID: UUID; let track: TitleType; let wasSkipped: Bool; let timestamp: Date }
interfaces.notes: Append-only comparison trail for auditability and future analytics-free troubleshooting.

interfaces.name: InsertionContext
interfaces.signature: @Model final class InsertionContext { var id: UUID; var titleID: UUID; var flowID: UUID; var mediaType: MediaType; var lo: Int; var hi: Int; var lastComparedTitleID: UUID?; var skipBudget: Int; var createdAt: Date; var updatedAt: Date }
interfaces.notes: FINAL: lastComparedTitleID is UUID?, not an index — both sides agreed in round 2. Single source of truth for crash-recoverable active insertion.

interfaces.name: TitleRepository
interfaces.signature: protocol TitleRepository { func fetchAll(track: MediaType?) async throws -> [Title]; func fetchRanked(track: MediaType) async throws -> [Title]; func title(id: UUID) async throws -> Title?; func add(name: String, mediaType: MediaType, year: Int?, genre: Genre?) async throws -> Title; func delete(_ id: UUID) async throws; func applyPositions(_ updates: [(id: UUID, position: Int)], track: MediaType) async throws; func capReached(track: MediaType) async throws -> Bool }
interfaces.notes: applyPositions is one atomic write per insertion, bulk shift unanimated. capReached folded in from Codex round 2 for the 25-item movie cap check.

interfaces.name: TitleFilter
interfaces.signature: struct TitleFilter: Equatable, Sendable { var genre: Genre?; var decade: Int? }
interfaces.notes: Decade derived from year at read time, never stored as free text.

interfaces.name: RankingEngine
interfaces.signature: enum RankingEngine { static func startInsertion(newID: UUID, rankedIDs: [UUID]) -> InsertionState; static func nextPair(state: InsertionState, rankedIDs: [UUID]) -> ComparisonPair?; static func applyChoice(state: InsertionState, chosenID: UUID, rankedIDs: [UUID]) -> InsertionStepResult; static func applySkip(state: InsertionState, rankedIDs: [UUID]) -> InsertionStepResult }
interfaces.notes: FINAL: static-func enum, both sides agreed. Lives in the standalone FaveOffRanking local package (Foundation-only) so a SwiftData/SwiftUI import here is a compile error.

interfaces.name: RankingStep
interfaces.signature: struct RankingStep: Sendable { let context: InsertionContext; let leftID: UUID; let rightID: UUID; let isTerminal: Bool }
interfaces.notes: Defines compare pair surfaced to UI when not terminal.

interfaces.name: RankingTransition
interfaces.signature: struct RankingTransition: Sendable { let updatedContext: InsertionContext?; let placedID: UUID?; let newPosition: Int?; let fallbackReason: SkipFallbackReason?; let neighbors: (UUID?, UUID?) }
interfaces.notes: Returned after each step; terminal transitions include placement payload and neighbors.

interfaces.name: SkipFallbackReason
interfaces.signature: enum SkipFallbackReason: String, Sendable { case twoSkips; case userCancelled; case deletedOpponent }
interfaces.notes: Explicit transparency for copy and analytics-safe behavior.

interfaces.name: MetadataProvider
interfaces.signature: protocol MetadataProvider { func enrich(title: FilmTitle) async -> PosterMetadata; }
interfaces.notes: Default implementation must be local/offline stub; remote provider optional and additive.

interfaces.name: PosterMetadata
interfaces.signature: struct PosterMetadata: Sendable { let resolvedName: String; let year: Int?; let genre: Genre?; let posterImageData: Data?; let posterColorHex: String; let textSeed: String }
interfaces.notes: Keep fallback deterministic from local stub.

interfaces.name: PurchaseService
interfaces.signature: protocol PurchaseService { var state: PurchaseState { get }; func loadProducts() async throws -> [PurchaseProduct]; func purchase(_ productID: ProductID) async throws; func restore() async throws; func isEntitled(to feature: PremiumFeature) -> Bool; func startEntitlementListener() async; func stopEntitlementListener() }
interfaces.notes: Both sides converged on this exact merged shape in round 2. startEntitlementListener runs Transaction.currentEntitlements as a persistent launch-time Task.

interfaces.name: PurchaseResult
interfaces.signature: enum PurchaseResult: Sendable { case purchased; case pending; case cancelled; case failed(Error) }
interfaces.notes: Sheet state machine should map from this to non-blocking UI transitions.

interfaces.name: TasteCardRenderer
interfaces.signature: @MainActor struct TasteCardRenderer { func renderCard(titles: [Title]) async throws -> Image }
interfaces.notes: Returns Image, matching TasteCardRenderState.ready(Image) directly. Only fires once every source poster image is confirmed loaded — no ImageRenderer snapshot race.

interfaces.name: LibraryViewModel
interfaces.signature: @Observable @MainActor final class LibraryViewModel { var titles: [Title]; var selectedTrack: MediaType; var activeFilters: FilterState; var pendingResume: ActiveInsertionFlow?; var isAtCap: Bool; func load() async; func addTitle(name: String, mediaType: MediaType, year: Int?, genre: Genre?) async throws -> ActiveInsertionFlow?; func removeTitle(_ id: UUID) async throws; func resumeFlow() async throws -> ActiveInsertionFlow? }
interfaces.notes: @Observable (not ObservableObject) for property-level invalidation. addTitle/resumeFlow both produce the identical ActiveInsertionFlow shape, matching the single-cover invariant.

interfaces.name: CompareFlowViewModel
interfaces.signature: final class CompareFlowViewModel: ObservableObject { @MainActor func loadNextPair() async; @MainActor func choose(_ id: UUID) async; @MainActor func skip() async; @MainActor func cancelBecauseDeletedOpponent() }
interfaces.notes: Single-screen ritual inside one full-screen cover; fetches both titles by persistent ID before each action.

interfaces.name: PaywallViewModel
interfaces.signature: @Observable @MainActor final class PaywallViewModel { var state: PurchaseState; var products: [PurchaseProduct]; var openFrom: PaywallTrigger; func loadProducts() async; func purchase(_ id: ProductID) async; func restore() async }
interfaces.notes: Dismissal handled by @Environment(\.dismiss) at the view layer, not a view-model method — the sheet owns its own presentation lifecycle. openFrom folded in from Codex round 2 to drive contextual copy.

interfaces.name: PrivacyManifestSpec
interfaces.signature: func buildPrivacyManifestEntry() -> [String: Any]
interfaces.notes: Adopted from Codex's round-1 addition as-is — CI-checked helper keeping xcprivacy fields explicit and stable.

interfaces.name: Title
interfaces.signature: @Model final class Title { var id: UUID; var name: String; var mediaType: MediaType; var year: Int?; var genre: Genre?; var posterColorSeed: String; var posterInitials: String; var rankingState: RankingState; var position: Int?; var createdAt: Date; var comparisonCount: Int }
interfaces.notes: FINAL: no separate domain/persistence DTO layer; used directly through TitleRepository. No @Relationship, no @Attribute(.unique) on id — deliberately dropped from Codex's round-2 draft since unique attributes break CloudKit-sync compatibility per ios_architecture_review; UUID() generation already gives practical uniqueness. Reference type despite kind=struct (schema has no 'class' option).

interfaces.name: Comparison
interfaces.signature: @Model final class Comparison { var id: UUID; var winnerID: UUID; var loserID: UUID; var mediaType: MediaType; var timestamp: Date; var wasSkipped: Bool; var flowID: UUID }
interfaces.notes: Append-only audit trail; stays valid after the referenced Title is deleted.

interfaces.name: MediaType
interfaces.signature: enum MediaType: String, Codable, CaseIterable, Sendable { case movie, tv }
interfaces.notes: Track discriminator; movie is free-ranked, tv is addable but ranking-gated until entitled.

interfaces.name: FaveOffSchemaV1
interfaces.signature: enum FaveOffSchemaV1: VersionedSchema { static var models: [any PersistentModel.Type] { [Title.self, Comparison.self, InsertionContext.self] }; static var versionIdentifier: Schema.Version { Schema.Version(1, 0, 0) } }

interfaces.name: FaveOffMigrationPlan

interfaces.name: ComparisonRepository
interfaces.signature: protocol ComparisonRepository { func record(winnerID: UUID, loserID: UUID, mediaType: MediaType, wasSkipped: Bool, flowID: UUID) async throws }
interfaces.notes: Kept as its own focused protocol, not folded into TitleRepository.

interfaces.name: InsertionRepository
interfaces.signature: protocol InsertionRepository { func pendingContext() async throws -> InsertionContext?; func save(_ context: InsertionContext) async throws; func clear(flowID: UUID) async throws }
interfaces.notes: pendingContext() drives the Library resume banner at launch.

interfaces.name: RepositoryError
interfaces.signature: enum RepositoryError: Error { case notFound; case notEntitled(PremiumFeature); case saveFailed(underlying: Error); case interrupted }
interfaces.notes: Merged: notEntitled case folded in from Codex's round-2 addition for feature-gated write attempts.

interfaces.name: InsertionState
interfaces.signature: struct InsertionState: Equatable, Codable, Sendable { var targetID: UUID; var lo: Int; var hi: Int; var lastComparedID: UUID?; var skipBudget: Int }
interfaces.notes: Pure in-memory mirror of InsertionContext; ID-based for the same drift reasons.

interfaces.name: ComparisonPair
interfaces.signature: struct ComparisonPair: Equatable, Sendable { var candidateID: UUID; var midIndex: Int }

interfaces.name: InsertionStepResult
interfaces.signature: enum InsertionStepResult: Equatable, Sendable { case continueComparing(InsertionState); case placed(index: Int, placement: PlacementKind) }
interfaces.notes: FINAL shape both sides converged on in round 2, replacing Codex's original RankingTransition/SkipFallbackReason — placement and cancellation are not representable on the same type.

interfaces.name: PlacementKind
interfaces.signature: enum PlacementKind: Equatable, Sendable { case exact(aboveID: UUID?, belowID: UUID?); case fallbackBottom; case coldStart }
interfaces.notes: Drives Placed screen's honest copy variant; never represents an interruption — see InterruptionReason.

interfaces.name: InsertionCoordinator

interfaces.name: InterruptionReason
interfaces.signature: enum InterruptionReason: String, Sendable { case titleDeleted, userCancelled }
interfaces.notes: Fully separate from PlacementKind by design.

interfaces.signature: protocol MetadataLookupService { func lookup(title: String, mediaType: MediaType, year: Int?) async -> MetadataResult }

interfaces.signature: struct MetadataResult: Sendable { var posterColorSeed: String; var posterInitials: String; var resolvedYear: Int?; var resolvedGenre: Genre? }

interfaces.name: OfflineMetadataProvider
interfaces.signature: struct OfflineMetadataProvider: MetadataLookupService { func lookup(title: String, mediaType: MediaType, year: Int?) async -> MetadataResult }
interfaces.notes: Deterministic hash-of-title-string, synchronous, no network — default happy-path implementation.

interfaces.signature: enum PremiumFeature: Sendable { case tvRanking, unlimitedMovieRanking }

interfaces.signature: struct PurchaseProduct: Identifiable, Sendable { var id: ProductID; var displayPrice: String; var period: String }

interfaces.signature: enum ContrastValidator { static func contrastRatio(_ fg: Color, _ bg: Color) -> Double; static func meetsAA(fg: Color, bg: Color, isLargeText: Bool) -> Bool }

interfaces.signature: @MainActor final class AppEnvironment { let titleRepository: TitleRepository; let comparisonRepository: ComparisonRepository; let insertionRepository: InsertionRepository; let insertionCoordinator: InsertionCoordinator; let metadataService: MetadataLookupService; let purchaseService: PurchaseService; let tasteCardRenderer: TasteCardRenderer }

interfaces.name: FilterState
